## **Volume Rendering of Neural Implicit Surfaces**

**Lior Yariv**[1] **Jiatao Gu**[2] **Yoni Kasten**[1] **Yaron Lipman**[1] _[,]_[2]

1Weizmann Institute of Science 2Facebook AI Research

## **Abstract**

Neural volume rendering became increasingly popular recently due to its success in synthesizing novel views of a scene from a sparse set of input images. So far, the geometry learned by neural volume rendering techniques was modeled using a generic density function. Furthermore, the geometry itself was extracted using an arbitrary level set of the density function leading to a noisy, often low fidelity reconstruction. The goal of this paper is to improve geometry representation and reconstruction in neural volume rendering. We achieve that by modeling the volume density as a function of the geometry. This is in contrast to previous work modeling the geometry as a function of the volume density. In more detail, we define the volume density function as Laplace’s cumulative distribution function (CDF) applied to a signed distance function (SDF) representation. This simple density representation has three benefits: (i) it provides a useful inductive bias to the geometry learned in the neural volume rendering process; (ii) it facilitates a bound on the opacity approximation error, leading to an accurate sampling of the viewing ray. Accurate sampling is important to provide a precise coupling of geometry and radiance; and (iii) it allows efficient unsupervised disentanglement of shape and appearance in volume rendering. Applying this new density representation to challenging scene multiview datasets produced high quality geometry reconstructions, outperforming relevant baselines. Furthermore, switching shape and appearance between scenes is possible due to the disentanglement of the two.

## **1 Introduction**

Volume rendering [18] is a set of techniques that renders volume _density_ in _radiance fields_ by the so called volume rendering integral. It has recently been shown that representing both the density and radiance fields as neural networks can lead to excellent prediction of novel views by learning only from a sparse set of input images. This neural volume rendering approach, presented in [21] and developed by its follow-ups [34, 2] approximates the integral as alpha-composition in a differentiable way, allowing to learn simultaneously both from input images. Although this coupling indeed leads to good generalization of novel viewing directions, the density part is not as successful in faithfully predicting the scene’s actual geometry, often producing noisy, low fidelity geometry approximation.

We propose VolSDF to devise a different model for the density in neural volume rendering, leading to better approximation of the scene’s geometry while maintaining the quality of view synthesis. The key idea is to represent the density as a function of the signed distance to the scene’s surface, see Figure 1. Such density function enjoys several benefits. First, it guarantees the existence of a well-defined surface that generates the density. This provides a useful inductive bias for disentangling density and radiance fields, which in turn provides a more accurate geometry approximation. Second, we show this density formulation allows bounding the approximation error of the opacity along rays. This bound is used to sample the viewing ray so to provide a faithful coupling of density and radiance field in the volume rendering integral. E.g., without such a bound the computed radiance along a ray (pixel color) can potentially miss or extend surface parts leading to incorrect radiance approximation.

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

Figure 1: VolSDF: given a set of input images (left) we learn a volumetric density (center-left, sliced) defined by a signed distance function (center-right, sliced) to produce a neural rendering (right). This definition of density facilitates high quality geometry reconstruction (gray surfaces, middle).

A closely related line of research, often referred to as neural implicit surfaces [22, 38, 14], have been focusing on representing the scene’s geometry implicitly using a neural network, making the surface rendering process differentiable. The main drawback of these methods is their requirement of masks that separate objects from the background. Also, learning to render surfaces directly tends to grow extraneous parts due to optimization problems, which are avoided by volume rendering. In a sense, our work combines the best of both worlds: _volume rendering with neural implicit surfaces_ .

We demonstrate the efficacy of VolSDF by reconstructing surfaces from the DTU [12] and BlendedMVS [37] datasets. VolSDF produces more accurate surface reconstructions compared to NeRF [21] and NeRF++ [39], and comparable reconstruction compared to IDR [38], while avoiding the use of object masks. Furthermore, we show disentanglement results with our method, i.e., switching the density and radiance fields of different scenes, which is shown to fail in NeRF-based models.

## **2 Related work**

**Neural Scene Representation & Rendering** Implicit functions are traditionally adopted in modeling 3D scenes [24, 11, 4]. Recent studies have been focusing on model implicit functions with multi-layer perceptron (MLP) due to its expressive representation power and low memory foot-print, including scene (geometry & appearance) representation [9, 20, 19, 23, 25, 29, 36, 28, 35] and free-view rendering [33, 16, 30, 26, 17, 21, 15, 39, 34, 2]. In particular, NeRF [21] has opened up a line of research (see [6] for an overview) combining neural implicit functions together with volume rendering to achieve photo-realistic rendering results. However, it is non-trivial to find a proper threshold to extract surfaces from the predicted density, and the recovered geometry is far from satisfactory. Furthermore, sampling of points along a ray for rendering a pixel is done using an opacity function that is approximated from another network without any guarantee for correct approximation.

**Multi-view 3D Reconstruction** Image-based 3D surface reconstruction (multi-view stereo) has been a longstanding problem in the past decades. Classical multi-view stereo approaches are generally either depth-based [1, 31, 8, 7] or voxel-based [5, 3, 32]. For instance, in COLMAP [31] (a typical depth-based method) image features are extracted and matched across different views to estimate depth. Then the predicted depth maps are fused to obtain dense point clouds. To obtain the surface, an additional meshing step e.g. Poisson surface reconstruction [13] is applied. However, these methods with complex pipelines may accumulate errors at each stage and usually result in incomplete 3D models, especially for non-Lambertian surfaces as they can not handle view dependent colors. On the contrary, although it produces complete models by directly modeling objects in a volume, voxel-based approaches are limited to low resolution due to high memory consumption. Recently, neural-based approaches such as DVR [22], IDR [38], NLR [14] have also been proposed to reconstruct scene geometry from multi-view images. However, these methods require accurate object masks and appropriate weight initialization due to the difficulty of propagating gradients.

Independently from and concurrently with our work here, [27] also use implicit surface representation incorporated into volume rendering. In particular, they replace the local transparency function with an occupancy network [19]. This allows adding surface smoothing term to the loss, improving the quality of the resulting surfaces. Differently from their approach, we use signed distance representation, regularized with an Eikonal loss [38, 10] without any explicit smoothing term. Furthermore, we show that the choice of using signed distance allows bounding the opacity approximation error, facilitating the approximation of the volume rendering integral for the suggested family of densities.

## **3 Method**

In this section we introduce a novel parameterization for volume density, defined as transformed signed distance function. Then we show how this definition facilitates the volume rendering process. In particular, we derive a bound of the error in the opacity approximation and consequently devise a sampling procedure for approximating the volume rendering integral.

## **3.1 Density as transformed SDF**

Let the set Ω _⊂_ R[3] represent the space occupied by some object in R[3] , and _M_ = _∂_ Ω its boundary surface. We denote by **1** Ω the Ω indicator function, and by _d_ Ω the Signed Distance Function (SDF) to its boundary _M_ ,

where _∥·∥_ is the standard Euclidean 2-norm. In neural volume rendering the volume _density σ_ : R[3] _→_ R+ is a scalar volumetric function, where _σ_ ( _**x**_ ) is the rate that light is occluded at point _**x**_ ; _σ_ is called density since it is proportional to the particle count per unit volume at _**x**_ [18]. In previous neural volumetric rendering approaches [21, 15, 39], the density function, _σ_ , was modeled with a general-purpose Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). In this work we suggest to model the density using a certain transformation of a learnable Signed Distance Function (SDF) _d_ Ω, namely

_σ_ ( _**x**_ ) = _α_ Ψ _β_ ( _−d_ Ω( _**x**_ )) _,_ (2)

where _α, β >_ 0 are learnable parameters, and Ψ _β_ is the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of the Laplace distribution with zero mean and _β_ scale (i.e., mean absolute deviation, which is intuitively the _L_ 1 version of the standard deviation),

Figure 1 (center left and right) depicts an example of such a density and SDF. As can be readily checked from this definition, as _β_ approach zero, the density _σ_ converges to a scaled indicator function of Ω, that is _σ → α_ **1** Ω for all points _**x** ∈_ Ω _\ M_ .

Intuitively, the density _σ_ models a homogeneous object with a constant density _α_ that smoothly decreases near the object’s boundary, where the smoothing amount is controlled by _β_ . The benefit in defining the density as in equation 2 is two-fold: First, it provides a useful inductive bias for the surface geometry _M_ , and provides a principled way to reconstruct the surface, i.e., as the zero level-set of _d_ Ω. This is in contrast to previous work where the reconstruction was chosen as an arbitrary level set of the learned density. Second, the particular form of the density as defined in equation 2 facilitates a bound on the error of the _opacity_ (or, equivalently the _transparency_ ) of the rendered volume, a crucial component in the volumetric rendering pipeline. In contrast, such a bound will be hard to devise for a generic MLP densities.

## **3.2 Volume rendering of** _σ_

In this section we review the volume rendering integral and the numerical integration commonly used to approximate it, requiring a set _S_ of sample points per ray. In the following section (Section 3.3), we explore the properties of the density _σ_ and derive a bound on the opacity approximation error along viewing rays. Finally, in Section 3.4 we derive an algorithm for producing a sample _S_ to be used in the volume rendering numerical integration.

In volume rendering we consider a ray _**x**_ emanating from a camera position _**c** ∈_ R[3] in direction _**v** ∈_ R[3] , _∥_ _**v** ∥_ = 1, defined by _**x**_ ( _t_ ) = _**c**_ + _t_ _**v**_ , _t ≥_ 0. In essence, volume rendering is all about approximating the integrated (i.e., summed) light radiance along this ray reaching the camera. There are two important quantities that participate in this computation: the volume’s _opacity O_ , or equivalently, its _transperancy T_ , and the _radiance field L_ .

The _transparency_ function of the volume along a ray _**x**_ , denoted _T_ , indicates, for each _t ≥_ 0, the probability a light particle succeeds traversing the segment [ _**c** ,_ _**x**_ ( _t_ )] without bouncing off,

**Sampling.** Since the PDF _τ_ is typically extremely concentrated near the object’s boundary (see e.g., Figure 3, right) the choice of the sample points _S_ has a crucial effect on the approximation quality of equation 8. One solution is to use an adaptive sample, e.g., _S_ computed with the inverse CDF, i.e., _O[−]_[1] . However, _O_ depends on the density model _σ_ and is not given explicitly. In [21] a second, coarse network was trained specifically for the approximation of the opacity _O_ , and was used for inverse sampling. However, the second network’s density does not necessarily faithfully represents the first network’s density, for which we wish to compute the volume integral. Furthermore, as we show later, one level of sampling could be insufficient to produce an accurate sample _S_ . Using a naive or crude approximation of _O_ would lead to a sub-optimal sample set _S_ that misses, or over extends non-negligible _τ_ values. Consequently, incorrect radiance approximations can occur (i.e., pixel color), potentially harming the learned density-radiance field decomposition. Our solution works with a single density _σ_ , and the sampling _S_ is computed by a sampling algorithm based on an error bound for the opacity approximation. ## **3.4 Sampling algorithm**

In this section we develop an algorithm for computing the sampling _S_ to be used in equation 8. This is done by first utilizing the bound in equation 15 to find samples _T_ so that _O_[�] (via equation 10) provides an _ϵ_ approximation to the true opacity _O_ , where _ϵ_ is a hyper-parameter, that is _BT ,β < ϵ_ . Second, we perform inverse CDF sampling with _O_[ˆ] , as described in Section 3.2.

Note that from Lemma 1 it follows that we can simply choose large enough _n_ to ensure _BT ,β < ϵ_ . However, this would lead to prohibitively large number of samples. Instead, we suggest a simple algorithm to reduce the number of required samples in practice and allows working with a limited budget of sample points. In a nutshell, we start with a uniform sampling _T_ = _T_ 0, and use Lemma 2 to initially set a _β_ + _> β_ that satisfies _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ . Then, we repeatedly upsample _T_ to reduce _β_ + while maintaining _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ . Even though this simple strategy is not guaranteed to converge, we find that _β_ + usually converges to _β_ (typically 85%, see also Figure 3), and even in cases it does not, the algorithm provides _β_ + for which the opacity approximation still maintains an _ϵ_ error. The algorithm is presented below (Algorithm 1).

We initialize _T_ (Line 1 in Algorithm 1) with uniform sampling _T_ 0 = _{ti}[n] i_ =1[, where] _[ t][k]_[= (] _[k][−]_[1)] _n[M] −_ 1[,] _k ∈_ [ _n_ ] (we use _n_ = 128 in our implementation). Given this sampling we next pick _β_ + _> β_ according to Lemma 2 so that the error bound satisfies the required _ϵ_ bound (Line 2 in Algorithm 1).

In order to reduce _β_ + while keep _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ , _n_ samples are added to _T_ (Line 4 in Algorithm 1), where the number of points sampled from each interval is proportional to its current error bound, equation 14. Assuming _T_ was sufficiently upsampled and satisfy _BT ,β_ + _< ϵ_ , we decrease _β_ + towards _β_ . Since the algorithm did not stop we have that _BT ,β > ϵ_ . Therefore the Mean Value Theorem implies the existence of _β⋆ ∈_ ( _β, β_ +) such that _BT ,β⋆_ = _ϵ_ . We use the bisection method (with maximum of 10 iterations) to efficiently search for _β⋆_ and update _β_ + accordingly (Lines 6 and 7 in Algorithm 1). The algorithm runs iteratively until _BT ,β ≤ ϵ_ or a maximal number of 5 iterations is reached. Either way, we use the final _T_ and _β_ + (guaranteed to provide _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ ) to estimate the current opacity _O_[�] , Line 10 in Algorithm 1). Finally we return a fresh set of _m_ = 64 samples _O_[ˆ] using inverse transform sampling (Line 11 in Algorithm 1). ## **3.5 Training**

Our system consists of two Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP): (i) _**f** ϕ_ approximating the SDF of the learned geometry, as well as global geometry feature _**z**_ of dimension 256, i.e., _**f** ϕ_ ( _**x**_ ) = ( _d_ ( _**x**_ ) _,_ _**z**_ ( _**x**_ )) _∈_ R[1+256] , where _ϕ_ denotes its learnable parameters; (ii) _Lψ_ ( _**x** ,_ _**n** ,_ _**v** ,_ _**z**_ ) _∈_ R[3] representing the scene’s radiance field with learnable parameters _ψ_ . In addition we have two scalar learnable parameters _α, β ∈_ R. In fact, in our implementation we make the choice _α_ = _β[−]_[1] . We denote by _θ ∈_ R _[p]_ the collection of all learnable parameters of the model, _θ_ = ( _ϕ, ψ, β_ ). To facilitate the learning of high frequency details of the geometry and radiance field, we exploit positional encoding [21] for the position _**x**_ and view direction _**v**_ in the geometry and radiance field. The influence of different positional encoding choices are presented in the supplementary.

Our data consists of a collection of images with camera parameters. From this data we extract pixel level data: for each pixel _p_ we have a triplet ( _Ip,_ _**c** p,_ _**v** p_ ), where _Ip ∈_ R[3] is its intensity (RGB color), _**c** p ∈_ R[3] is its camera location, and _**v** p ∈_ R[3] is the viewing direction (camera to pixel). We train with batches of size 1024 pixels _p_ . _λ_ is a hyper-parameter set to 0 _._ 1 throughout the the experiments. Further implementation details are provided in the supplementary.

## **4 Experiments**

We evaluate our method on the challenging task of multiview 3D surface reconstruction. We use two datasets: DTU [12] and BlendedMVS [37], both containing real objects with different materials that are captured from multiple views. In Section 4.1 we show qualitative and quantitative 3D surface reconstruction results of VolSDF, comparing favorably to relevant baselines. In Section 4.2 we demonstrate that, in contrast to NeRF [21], our model is able to successfully disentangle the geometry and appearance of the captured objects.

## **4.1 Multi-view 3D reconstruction**

**DTU** The DTU [12] dataset contains multi-view image (49 or 64) of different objects with fixed camera and lighting parameters. We evaluate our method on the 15 scans that were selected by [38]. We compare our surface accuracy using the Chamfer _l_ 1 loss (measured in mm) to COLMAP0 (which is watertight reconstruction; COLMAP7 is not watertight and provided only for reference) [31], NeRF [21] and IDR [38], where for fair comparison with IDR we only evaluate the reconstruction inside the visual hull of the objects (defined by the segmentation masks of [38]). We further evaluate the PSNR of our rendering compared to [21]. Quantitative results are presented in Table 1. It can be observed that our method is on par with IDR (that uses object masks for all images) and outperforms NeRF and COLMAP in terms of reconstruction accuracy. Our rendering quality is comparable to NeRF’s.

**BlendedMVS** The BlendedMVS dataset [37] contains a large collection of 113 scenes captured from multiple views. It supplies high quality ground truth 3D models for evaluation, various camera configurations, and a variety of indoor/outdoor real environments. We selected 9 different scenes and used our method to reconstruct the surface of each object. In contrast to the DTU dataset, BlendedMVS scenes have complex backgrounds. Therefore we use NeRF++ [39] as a baseline for this dataset. In Table 2 we present our results compared to NeRF++. Qualitative comparisons are presented in Fig. 5; since the units are unknown in this case we present relative improvement of Chamfer distance (in %) compared to NeRF. Also in this case, we improve NeRF reconstructions considerably, while being on-par in terms of the rendering quality (PSNR).

**Comparison to [38]** IDR [38] is the state of the art 3D surface reconstruction method using implicit representation. However, it suffers from two drawbacks: first, it requires object masks for training, which is a strong supervision signal. Second, since it sets the pixel color based only on the single point of intersection of the corresponding viewing ray, it is more pruned to local minima that Figure 6: IDR extraneous parts. sometimes appear in the form of extraneous surface parts. ## **4.2 Disentanglement of geometry and appearance**

We have tested the disentanglement of scenes to geometry (density) and appearance (radiance field) by switching the radiance fields of two trained scenes. For VolSDF we switched _Lψ_ . For NeRF [21] we note that the radiance field is computed as _Lψ_ ( _**z** ,_ _**v**_ ), where _Lψ_ is a fully connected network with one hidden layer (of width 128 and ReLU activation) and _**z**_ is a feature vector. We tested two versions of NeRF disentanglement: First, by switching the original radiance fields _Lψ_ of trained NeRF networks. Second, by switching the radiance fields of trained NeRF models with an identical radiance field model to ours, namely _Lψ_ ( _**x** ,_ _**n** ,_ _**v** ,_ _**z**_ ). As shown in Figure 7 both versions of NeRF fail to produce a correct disentanglement in these scenes, while VolSDF successfully switches the materials of the two objects. We attribute this to the specific inductive bias injected with the use of the density in equation 2.

## **5 Conclusions**

We introduce VolSDF, a volume rendering framework for implicit neural surfaces. We represent the volume density as a transformed version of the signed distance function to the learned surface geometry. This seemingly simple definition provides a useful inductive bias, allowing disentanglement of geometry (i.e., density) and radiance field, and improves the geometry approximation over previous neural volume rendering techniques. Furthermore, it allows to bound the opacity approximation error leading to high fidelity sampling of the volume rendering integral.

Some limitations of our method present interesting future research opportunities. First, although working well in practice, we do not have a proof of correctness for the sampling algorithm. We believe providing such a proof, or finding a version of this algorithm that has a proof would be a useful contribution. In general, we believe working with bounds in volume rendering could improve learning and disentanglement and push the field forward. Second, representing non-watertight manifolds and/or manifolds with boundaries, such as zero thickness surfaces, is not possible with an SDF. Generalizations such as multiple implicits and unsigned fields could be proven valuable. Third, our current formulation assumes homogeneous density; extending it to more general density models would allow representing a broader class of geometries. Fourth, now that high quality geometries can be learned in an unsupervised manner it will be interesting to learn dynamic geometries and shape spaces directly from collections of images. Lastly, although we don’t see immediate negative societal impact of our work, we do note that accurate geometry reconstruction from images can be used for malice purposes.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents VolSDF, a well-motivated contribution that addresses a clear limitation in neural volume rendering: the poor geometric fidelity of density-based representations like NeRF. The core idea—modeling volume density as a Laplace CDF applied to a signed distance function—is elegant and theoretically grounded, and the authors articulate three concrete benefits (inductive bias for geometry, bounded opacity approximation error enabling principled sampling, and shape/appearance disentanglement) that give the paper a clear narrative arc. The introduction is generally well-written and the positioning relative to both neural volume rendering (NeRF, NeRF++) and neural implicit surfaces (IDR, DVR, NLR) is appropriate, with the key selling point being that VolSDF combines the strengths of both paradigms while eliminating the need for object masks required by surface-rendering approaches. The most significant concern at this stage is the paper's relationship to the concurrent work of [27] (presumably NeuS), which pursues a very similar SDF-to-density reformulation; while the authors distinguish their approach via the Eikonal regularization and the opacity error bound, the latter distinction carries much of the novelty weight and should be rigorously demonstrated—both theoretically and empirically—in the body of the paper. Additional minor issues include: the claim of "comparable" reconstruction to IDR is somewhat vague and invites scrutiny about whether removing masks actually costs accuracy; the disentanglement contribution feels somewhat tacked-on relative to the geometry focus; and the abstract/intro would benefit from quantitative teasers. Overall, the premise is strong and timely, but the paper's lasting impact will hinge on how convincingly the theoretical sampling guarantee translates into measurable quality gains over both NeRF-family and concurrent SDF-based volume renderers.

comments.quote: Ψ _β_ is the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of the Laplace distribution with zero mean and _β_ scale (i.e., mean absolute deviation, which is intuitively the _L_ 1 version of the standard deviation)
comments.explanation: The parenthetical identifies the scale parameter β of the Laplace distribution with the mean absolute deviation. For a Laplace distribution with zero mean and scale b, the mean absolute deviation E[|X|] equals b, so this is actually correct — the scale parameter of the Laplace distribution does coincide with its MAD. progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a strong and timely contribution: replacing unconstrained NeRF-style density fields with an SDF-induced density is well motivated, clearly connected to the goal of improving geometric reconstruction, and the claimed advantages—better inductive bias, opacity-error-controlled sampling, and shape/appearance disentanglement—are potentially significant. The introduction is generally clear and positions the work effectively between neural volume rendering and neural implicit surface methods, making the central idea easy to understand even before the technical sections. That said, the paper’s opening sometimes overstates claims before fully justifying them: statements such as “guarantees the existence of a well-defined surface,” “accurate sampling,” and “unsupervised disentanglement” need careful qualification and empirical/theoretical support, especially since the practical behavior will depend on optimization, SDF regularization, scene assumptions, and network capacity. The comparison to prior work is compelling but could be more balanced: methods requiring masks are criticized, while the assumptions and limitations of VolSDF—camera calibration, bounded scenes, foreground/background handling, thin structures, non-Lambertian effects, and failure cases—are not yet acknowledged. The related work is adequate but somewhat terse and occasionally imprecise in phrasing, and the distinction from concurrent work should be made more concretely once the method is introduced. Overall, the paper appears high quality and likely important, with a clear conceptual contribution, but its most significant issues are the need to substantiate broad claims, clarify assumptions and limitations, and ensure that experimental comparisons isolate the benefits of the SDF-density formulation from differences in sampling, regularization, and implementation details.

comments.title: Previous work is slightly mischaracterized as explicitly modeling geometry as a function of density
comments.quote: We achieve that by modeling the volume density as a function of the geometry. This is in contrast to previous work modeling the geometry as a function of the volume density.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is valid: VolSDF defines density from an SDF whose zero level set is the surface, whereas NeRF-style methods learn a generic density and then extract geometry post hoc. However, the phrasing overstates the symmetry between the approaches. Most prior neural volume-rendering methods do not explicitly model geometry as a mathematical function of density during training; geometry is inferred or extracted after training from the learned density field.

comments.title: Disentanglement claims are stronger than what the architecture and experiments isolate
comments.quote: This simple density representation has three benefits: ... (iii) it allows efficient unsupervised disentanglement of shape and appearance in volume rendering. / We have tested the disentanglement of scenes to geometry (density) and appearance (radiance field) by switching the radiance fields of two trained scenes. For VolSDF we switched Lψ. / We attribute this to the specific inductive bias injected with the use of the density in equation 2.
comments.explanation: The paper plausibly shows that the SDF-based density representation encourages a cleaner geometry/appearance separation than a generic NeRF density. However, the disentanglement is attributed too directly to the density parameterization alone. VolSDF also differs from NeRF in its Eikonal regularization, normal definition, sampling procedure, radiance architecture, feature interface, and training dynamics. In addition, the radiance network is conditioned on the geometry-network feature z(x), which is not constrained to contain only geometry and could carry appearance or material information. Therefore, switching only Lψ is a useful qualitative probe, but it does not cleanly establish that appearance is entirely contained in the radiance field or that the density transform alone causes the disentanglement.

comments.title: Volume rendering is described as avoiding extraneous surface-growth artifacts too broadly
comments.quote: Also, learning to render surfaces directly tends to grow extraneous parts due to optimization problems, which are avoided by volume rendering.
comments.explanation: Volume rendering can mitigate some optimization difficulties of direct surface rendering because it integrates along rays rather than relying on hard surface intersections. But the statement is too categorical: generic neural volume-rendering methods can still produce floaters, spurious semi-transparent structures, noisy density, and other geometry artifacts. A more precise claim would be that volume rendering alleviates some surface-rendering optimization artifacts, not that it avoids extraneous geometry in general.

comments.title: NeRF-style hierarchical sampling is imprecisely described as using a separate opacity-approximation network
comments.quote: Furthermore, sampling of points along a ray for rendering a pixel is done using an opacity function that is approximated from another network without any guarantee for correct approximation.
comments.explanation: The criticism is directionally reasonable: NeRF's coarse network supplies importance-sampling weights, and there is no formal guarantee that these weights accurately match the final fine network's opacity. However, standard NeRF does not train a separate network whose explicit purpose is to approximate an opacity function. It trains a coarse radiance/density field with the rendering loss, then derives sampling weights from its predicted densities and transmittance. The wording could mislead readers into thinking prior work contains a dedicated opacity-approximation network with a separate opacity objective.

comments.title: Opacity is treated as a proper CDF, but finite-ray opacity may be less than one
comments.quote: assuming that every ray is eventually occluded O(∞) = 1. In that sense we can think of O as a CDF ... / Second, we perform inverse CDF sampling with Ô, as described in Section 3.2.
comments.explanation: The CDF interpretation is valid only under the stated assumption that every ray is eventually occluded. But for a bounded object or a ray over a finite interval [0,M], the accumulated opacity can be less than one, especially for background rays or weak intersections. Then O or Ô is a sub-CDF rather than a normalized CDF. Inverse-transform sampling would require an additional convention, such as normalization by Ô(M), adding a background point mass, sampling only on [0,Ô(M)], or specifying how flat regions are handled. The text states the simplifying assumption but does not explain how the practical finite-interval algorithm handles rays whose total opacity is below one.

comments.title: The adaptive quadrature and upsampling procedure is underspecified for exact reproduction
comments.quote: The integral in equation 7 is approximated using a numerical quadrature, namely the rectangle rule, at some discrete samples S ... / n samples are added to T ... where the number of points sampled from each interval is proportional to its current error bound
comments.explanation: The high-level sampling strategy is clear, but several numerical details are left implicit. The sample set appears to include both endpoints of [0,M], which creates an off-by-one ambiguity for a rectangle rule unless the interval weights and final interval convention are specified. The text also uses a generic Δs even though later sampling may be nonuniform. For adaptive upsampling, it does not specify how proportional interval allocations are rounded to exactly n samples, where within each interval new samples are placed, or how ties and zero-error intervals are handled. These choices affect the sample set and practical convergence, so the printed algorithm is not fully reproducible without supplementary details or code.

comments.title: The sampling guarantee is conditional on an exact SDF, while the learned network is only regularized toward one
comments.quote: The proof of this theorem, which is provided in the supplementary, makes a principled use of the signed distance function’s unique properties;
comments.explanation: The theorem is well motivated for a true signed distance function, whose geometric and Lipschitz properties can be used to bound density variation along a ray. In the implemented model, however, the SDF is represented by an MLP and encouraged with an Eikonal loss; this does not guarantee that the learned field is an exact SDF everywhere or that it satisfies the exact geometric assumptions used in the proof. The guarantee should therefore be read as applying to the idealized SDF model, while the learned model relies on regularization and empirical behavior.

comments.title: The β+ sampling guarantee may apply to a modified density rather than the current model density
comments.quote: Either way, we use the final T and β+ (guaranteed to provide BT,β+ ≤ ϵ) to estimate the current opacity O, Line 10 in Algorithm 1).
comments.explanation: If the adaptive procedure terminates with β+ equal to the current β, the stated bound applies to the current density. But the text allows the algorithm to stop at max_iter with β+ > β. In that case, the guarantee BT,β+ ≤ ε controls the rectangle-rule error for the smoother density parameterized by β+, not necessarily for the actual current density using β. The prose nevertheless says the algorithm estimates the current opacity. Unless rendering deliberately uses β+ and distinguishes this from the model density, the guarantee is overstated or ambiguous.

comments.title: The α and β parameterization is inconsistent about positivity, learnability, and the bound notation
comments.quote: Taking the maximum over all intervals furnishes a bound BT,β as a function of T and β ... / In addition we have two scalar learnable parameters α, β ∈ R. In fact, in our implementation we make the choice α = β−1. We denote by θ ... θ = (φ, ψ, β).
comments.explanation: The density definition and opacity-error bounds require α and β to be positive. As written, saying α,β ∈ R would allow β ≤ 0, for which the Laplace scale, α = β−1, and the sampling bounds are not well defined. There is also an internal ambiguity: the text first presents α and β as two learnable scalars, then ties α deterministically to β and excludes α from θ. This also affects the notation BT,β: if α is independent, the opacity-error bound depends on α as well as β; if α = β−1, that dependence should be stated before suppressing α from the notation.

comments.title: The Eikonal-loss sampling notation and uniform-sampling domain are ambiguous
comments.quote: LSDF is the Eikonal loss encouraging d to approximate a signed distance function; the samples z are taken to combine a single random uniform space point and a single point from S for each pixel p.
comments.explanation: Earlier, z denotes the learned geometry feature output by the SDF network, not a spatial point. In the Eikonal loss, the samples must be 3D spatial locations where ∇d is evaluated. This may simply be a notation error for x, but as written it overloads an important symbol. In addition, the phrase “a single random uniform space point” does not specify the domain of the uniform distribution, such as a scene bounding box, unit sphere/cube, camera frustum, or object-centric region. That domain can materially affect the SDF regularization away from observed rays.

comments.title: Claim of outperforming COLMAP is ambiguous given the two COLMAP variants
comments.quote: It can be observed that our method is on par with IDR (that uses object masks for all images) and outperforms NeRF and COLMAP in terms of reconstruction accuracy.
comments.explanation: The paragraph distinguishes COLMAP0, described as watertight, from COLMAP7, described as not watertight and included for reference. The authors likely intend the main comparison to be against COLMAP0, for which VolSDF has a better mean Chamfer distance. But the unqualified phrase “COLMAP” is ambiguous because the table also reports COLMAP7, whose mean Chamfer distance is better than VolSDF's. The text should specify which COLMAP variant is being compared.

comments.title: BlendedMVS comparison alternates between NeRF++ and NeRF
comments.quote: Therefore we use NeRF++ [39] as a baseline for this dataset. In Table 2 we present our results compared to NeRF++. Qualitative comparisons are presented in Fig. 5; since the units are unknown in this case we present relative improvement of Chamfer distance (in %) compared to NeRF.
comments.explanation: The setup identifies NeRF++ as the relevant BlendedMVS baseline, which is sensible for scenes with complex backgrounds. The final sentence then says the relative Chamfer improvement is compared to NeRF. This may be a typographical omission of “++”, but as written the baseline for the percentage improvements is ambiguous, especially because the table reports only relative percentages rather than absolute Chamfer distances.

comments.title: The NeRF “with normal” baseline is underspecified because standard NeRF has no SDF normal
comments.quote: Second, by switching the radiance fields of trained NeRF models with an identical radiance field model to ours, namely Lψ(x, n, v, z).
comments.explanation: The comparison appears intended to control for the radiance-field architecture by giving NeRF a VolSDF-like color head. However, in VolSDF, n is the SDF level-set normal ∇d, which standard NeRF does not provide. The paper does not state whether the NeRF baseline uses density-gradient normals, normals from an extracted isosurface, some other proxy, or omits the normal. These choices are not equivalent and affect both fairness and reproducibility of the comparison.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a strong and timely contribution: replacing unconstrained NeRF-style density fields with an SDF-induced density is well motivated, clearly connected to the goal of improving geometric reconstruction, and the claimed advantages—better inductive bias, opacity-error-controlled sampling, and shape/appearance disentanglement—are potentially significant. The introduction is generally clear and positions the work effectively between neural volume rendering and neural implicit surface methods, making the central idea easy to understand even before the technical sections. That said, the paper’s opening sometimes overstates claims before fully justifying them: statements such as “guarantees the existence of a well-defined surface,” “accurate sampling,” and “unsupervised disentanglement” need careful qualification and empirical/theoretical support, especially since the practical behavior will depend on optimization, SDF regularization, scene assumptions, and network capacity. The comparison to prior work is compelling but could be more balanced: methods requiring masks are criticized, while the assumptions and limitations of VolSDF—camera calibration, bounded scenes, foreground/background handling, thin structures, non-Lambertian effects, and failure cases—are not yet acknowledged. The related work is adequate but somewhat terse and occasionally imprecise in phrasing, and the distinction from concurrent work should be made more concretely once the method is introduced. Overall, the paper appears high quality and likely important, with a clear conceptual contribution, but its most significant issues are the need to substantiate broad claims, clarify assumptions and limitations, and ensure that experimental comparisons isolate the benefits of the SDF-density formulation from differences in sampling, regularization, and implementation details.

comments.title: Previous work is slightly mischaracterized as explicitly modeling geometry as a function of density
comments.quote: We achieve that by modeling the volume density as a function of the geometry. This is in contrast to previous work modeling the geometry as a function of the volume density.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: VolSDF defines density from an SDF whose zero level set is the surface, whereas NeRF-style methods learn a generic density and then extract geometry post hoc, typically by thresholding or choosing a level set. That contrast is real and is later made more precise in the paper. What remains slightly problematic is the phrasing that previous work was "modeling the geometry as a function of the volume density." In most neural volume rendering methods, geometry is not explicitly modeled as a mathematical function of density during training; rather, geometry is inferred or extracted after training from a learned density field. A careful reader will likely infer the intended meaning from the surrounding sentence about arbitrary level sets, but the statement overstates the formal symmetry between the two approaches.

comments.title: Disentanglement is attributed too directly to the density parameterization alone
comments.quote: This simple density representation has three benefits: (i) it provides a useful inductive bias to the geometry learned in the neural volume rendering process; (ii) it facilitates a bound on the opacity approximation error, leading to an accurate sampling of the viewing ray. Accurate sampling is important to provide a precise coupling of geometry and radiance; and (iii) it allows efficient unsupervised disentanglement of shape and appearance in volume rendering.
comments.explanation: The first two benefits are well aligned with the later technical development: the SDF-based density gives a principled surface and enables an opacity-error bound. For the third, I can see the authors' intent from the full context: using an SDF-derived density and a radiance field conditioned on normals can make swapping density/shape and appearance fields more meaningful than in a generic-density NeRF. However, the wording says the density representation itself "allows" unsupervised disentanglement. Later sections indicate that disentanglement also depends on architectural choices, the radiance-field parameterization, normal conditioning, and empirical behavior under training, not solely on defining density as a transformed SDF. The claim is plausible as a high-level summary, but it is somewhat stronger than what the stated mechanism alone guarantees.

comments.title: Volume rendering is described as avoiding extraneous surface-growth artifacts too broadly
comments.quote: Also, learning to render surfaces directly tends to grow extraneous parts due to optimization problems, which are avoided by volume rendering.
comments.explanation: The authors are pointing to a real difficulty of differentiable surface rendering: visibility and correspondence gradients can be brittle, and mask-based surface methods can produce spurious geometry. Volume rendering often gives smoother optimization because it integrates along rays rather than relying on a single hard surface intersection. That said, the phrase "which are avoided by volume rendering" is too categorical. The same introduction and later discussion acknowledge that generic neural volume rendering can produce noisy, low-fidelity geometry and artifacts; NeRF-style densities can also contain floaters or spurious semi-transparent structures. A more precise claim would be that volume rendering mitigates some optimization issues of direct surface rendering, not that it avoids extraneous geometry in general.

comments.title: NeRF-style hierarchical sampling is described as using an opacity function approximated by another network
comments.quote: Furthermore, sampling of points along a ray for rendering a pixel is done using an opacity function that is approximated from another network without any guarantee for correct approximation.
comments.explanation: I understand the motivation: in NeRF, a coarse network is used to produce weights along the ray, and those weights define an importance-sampling distribution for additional fine samples. There is indeed no formal guarantee that this proposal distribution accurately captures the final fine network's opacity or transmittance. However, the description is somewhat imprecise. Standard NeRF does not train a separate network whose explicit purpose is to approximate an opacity function; it trains a coarse radiance/density field with the same rendering loss, and derives sampling weights from its predicted densities and transmittance. The statement is directionally correct as a critique of unguided hierarchical sampling, but it could mislead readers into thinking prior work contains a dedicated opacity-approximation network with a separately defined opacity objective.

comments.title: Opacity is treated as a normalized CDF under an assumption not guaranteed by the proposed density
comments.quote:
Note that _O_ is a monotonic increasing function where _O_ (0) = 0, and assuming that every ray is eventually occluded _O_ ( _∞_ ) = 1. In that sense we can think of _O_ as a CDF, and

is its Probability Density Function (PDF). The volume rendering equation is the expected light along the ray,
comments.explanation: The monotonicity and the relation between transparency and opacity are standard, and I understand the authors' intent: if the accumulated optical thickness along the ray is infinite, then the opacity integrates to one and the opacity derivative can be used as a probability density for where the ray terminates. However, this normalization is not generally implied by the density model introduced just above. For a bounded object with finite density scale α, a ray that intersects the object only over a finite chord has finite optical thickness, so O(∞)=1−exp(−∫σ)<1; a ray missing the object may have even smaller total opacity. Even with the Laplace-CDF SDF density, the exterior tails need not make the integral diverge. Thus τ is generally a sub-density unless an opaque background, an enclosing volume, normalization, or a background color/transmittance term is added. The text does state the assumption explicitly, so the issue is not a hidden algebraic error, but it is a substantive modeling assumption that is not justified here and is in tension with the earlier description of Ω as an object volume. Since the subsequent inverse-CDF sampling and 'expected light' interpretation rely on treating O as a proper CDF, a careful reader could be misled about when the derivation applies.

comments.title: Rectangle-rule sample set includes both endpoints but the interval weights are left implicit
comments.quote:
The integral in equation 7 is approximated using a numerical quadrature, namely the rectangle rule, at some discrete samples _S_ = _{si}[m] i_ =1[,][ 0 =] _[ s]_[1] _[< s]_[2] _[< . . . < s][m]_[=] _[ M]_[, where] _[ M]_[is some large constant:]

where we use the subscript _S_ in _I_[ˆ] _S_ to highlight the dependence of the approximation on the sample set _S_ , _τ_ ˆ _i ≈ τ_ ( _si_ )∆ _s_ is the approximated PDF multiplied by the interval length,
comments.explanation: The overall intent is clear: approximate the volume-rendering integral by alpha-compositing/rectangle-rule quadrature over samples along a ray, and the authors note that fuller details are in the supplementary. Still, as written, the sample set contains m points including both 0 and M. A left-rectangle rule on [0,M] with endpoints normally has m−1 intervals and would use samples s_i with weights Δ_i=s_{i+1}−s_i for i=1,…,m−1; if the sum instead runs over m samples, one needs either an additional endpoint s_{m+1}, a convention for the last interval, or a different quadrature convention. The prose also writes a generic Δs although the later development uses nonuniform intervals. This may well be clarified in the omitted equation or supplementary, so it is not necessarily an error in the method, but the passage itself leaves an off-by-one/weighting ambiguity that matters for reproducing the numerical approximation.

comments.title: Opacity-bound notation suppresses the density-scale parameter
comments.quote: Taking the maximum over all intervals furnishes a bound _BT ,β_ as a function of _T_ and _β_ ,
comments.explanation: The preceding discussion correctly notes that the derivative bound depends on the density parameters \(\alpha,\beta\), and this is also expected from the density definition \(\sigma(x)=\alpha\Psi_\beta(-d(x))\). I first wondered whether \(\alpha\) was being treated as fixed and therefore omitted from the notation. Later, Section 3.5 says the implementation uses \(\alpha=\beta^{-1}\), which would partly resolve this for the implemented method. However, in this section \(\alpha\) has still been introduced as an independent learnable parameter, and Lemma 2 / Algorithm 1 take only \(\beta\) as input. As written, the bound \(B_{T,\beta}\) is not only a function of \(T\) and \(\beta\) unless \(\alpha\) is fixed, tied to \(\beta\), or otherwise absorbed into the definition. This is a localized notation/method-description ambiguity, but it matters because changing \(\alpha\) changes the opacity and the rectangle-rule error.

comments.title: The claimed sampling guarantee is conditional on an exact SDF, but the learned network is only regularized toward one
comments.quote: The proof of this theorem, which is provided in the supplementary, makes a principled use of the signed distance function’s unique properties;
comments.explanation: The theoretical development is well motivated: for a true signed distance function, one can use its geometric structure and Lipschitz properties to bound the density variation along a ray. My concern is not with the conditional theorem itself, but with how strongly the later text describes the resulting approximation as guaranteed. In the actual model, Section 3.5 says an MLP approximates the SDF and is regularized by an Eikonal loss; such a network is not guaranteed to be an exact SDF everywhere, nor to satisfy the exact geometric endpoint-ball argument used in Theorem 1. The paper may intend the guarantee to hold for the idealized SDF model, with the implementation relying on the inductive bias and regularization. But the passage does not make that qualification explicit, so statements about a guaranteed \(\epsilon\)-approximation in the practical sampling algorithm could be read as stronger than what is justified for the learned field.

comments.title: Using \(\beta^+\) appears to guarantee the opacity of a modified density, not necessarily the current true opacity
comments.quote: Either way, we use the final _T_ and _β_ + (guaranteed to provide _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ ) to estimate the current opacity _O_[] , Line 10 in Algorithm 1).
comments.explanation: I understand the intent: \(\beta^+\) is introduced as a safer, smoother value for which the opacity approximation bound can be kept below \(\epsilon\), and the algorithm tries to reduce \(\beta^+\) toward the actual \(\beta\). If the loop succeeds with \(B_{T,\beta}\le\epsilon\), the guarantee is aligned with the current density. The problematic case is explicitly allowed by the text: the algorithm may stop at max_iter with \(\beta^+>\beta\). Then the bound \(B_{T,\beta^+}\le\epsilon\) controls the rectangle-rule error for the smoothed density parameterized by \(\beta^+\), not obviously for the actual density using \(\beta\). Yet the prose says it estimates the “current opacity” and earlier says the algorithm finds samples so that \(\hat O\) approximates the true opacity \(O\). Unless the rendering also deliberately uses \(\beta^+\) for opacity/sampling in a way that is formally distinguished from the model density, this is an overstatement or at least an ambiguity in the guarantee.

comments.title: Inverse-CDF sampling is underspecified when finite-ray opacity is less than one
comments.quote: Second, we perform inverse CDF sampling with _O_[ˆ] , as described in Section 3.2.
comments.explanation: The idea of sampling from the opacity CDF is standard and sensible when \(O\) is a proper CDF ending at one. The surrounding context even states the simplifying assumption that every ray is eventually occluded, so \(O(\infty)=1\). However, the numerical algorithm works on a finite interval \([0,M]\), and in SDF-based object rendering many rays may have \(\hat O(M)<1\), especially background rays or rays that only weakly intersect the density field. In that case \(\hat O\) on \([0,M]\) is a sub-CDF rather than a normalized CDF, and inverse-transform sampling requires an additional convention: normalize by \(\hat O(M)\), include a point mass at the far/background event, sample uniforms on \([0,\hat O(M)]\), or handle flat regions in some specified way. The text does not state which convention is used. This does not invalidate the bound itself, but it leaves an important implementation detail ambiguous for reproducing the sampling procedure.

comments.title: Upsampling rule lacks enough detail to reproduce the algorithm exactly
comments.quote: n samples are added to _T_ (Line 4 in Algorithm 1), where the number of points sampled from each interval is proportional to its current error bound, equation 14.
comments.explanation: The high-level adaptive strategy is clear: allocate more samples to intervals that currently contribute more to the opacity-error bound. I checked whether the surrounding text resolves the details, but the passage does not specify how proportional allocations are rounded to an integer total of exactly \(n\), where within each selected interval the new samples are placed, or how ties / zero-error intervals are handled. These choices can affect the resulting set \(T\), the subsequent bound value, and the practical convergence behavior. This is a fixable reproducibility gap rather than a conceptual flaw, especially if the supplementary material or code gives the missing details, but the algorithm as printed is underspecified.

comments.title: Positivity and learnability of the density parameters are inconsistent
comments.quote: In addition we have two scalar learnable parameters _α, β ∈_ R. In fact, in our implementation we make the choice _α_ = _β[−]_[1] . We denote by _θ ∈_ R _[p]_ the collection of all learnable parameters of the model, _θ_ = ( _ϕ, ψ, β_ ).
comments.explanation: The earlier density definition and the opacity-error bounds require α, β > 0: β is the Laplace scale and α is the density scale. I first checked whether this sentence is just saying that the unconstrained optimization variables are real-valued, with a later positive transform, but the passage does not state such a transform. As written, β ∈ R would allow β ≤ 0, for which the Laplace CDF scale, α = β^{-1}, and the sampling bounds are not well-defined. There is also a smaller internal inconsistency: the text first calls both α and β learnable parameters, but then fixes α = β^{-1} and excludes α from θ. That implementation choice is legitimate, but it should be described as tying α deterministically to a positive β, not as learning two independent real scalars.

comments.title: The Eikonal-loss sample variable conflicts with the feature notation
comments.quote: _L_ SDF is the Eikonal loss encouraging _d_ to approximate a signed distance function [10]; the samples _**z**_ are taken to combine a single random uniform space point and a single point from _S_ for each pixel _p_ .
comments.explanation: Earlier in the same paragraph, **z** is introduced as a 256-dimensional geometry feature output by the SDF network, **f**ϕ(**x**) = (d(**x**), **z**(**x**)). In the Eikonal loss, however, the samples must be spatial 3D points at which ∇d is evaluated, not 256-dimensional feature vectors. I considered whether this is an OCR error for **x**, which would fully resolve the issue. But if the intended paper notation really uses **z** here, it overloads an already important symbol in a way that can mislead readers about what distribution the Eikonal expectation is over. A different symbol for spatial samples, or an explicit clarification, would avoid the ambiguity.

comments.title: Eikonal uniform sampling domain is underspecified in the main method description
comments.quote: the samples _**z**_ are taken to combine a single random uniform space point and a single point from _S_ for each pixel _p_ .
comments.explanation: The overall idea is standard: Eikonal regularization is applied both off-surface/volumetric samples and ray samples. However, even assuming the sampled variable should be a spatial point rather than the feature **z**, the phrase “a single random uniform space point” leaves open the domain of the uniform distribution: the full scene bounding box, a normalized unit sphere/cube, the camera frustum, or some object-centric region. This matters because the Eikonal loss can strongly affect SDF behavior away from observed rays. The following sentence says that further implementation details are in the supplementary, so this may be resolved there, but in the passage itself a key reproducibility detail of the training objective is left implicit.

comments.title: Claim of outperforming COLMAP is ambiguous given the two COLMAP variants
comments.quote: It can be observed that our method is on par with IDR (that uses object masks for all images) and outperforms NeRF and COLMAP in terms of reconstruction accuracy.
comments.explanation: The paragraph carefully distinguishes COLMAP0, described as watertight, from COLMAP7, described as not watertight and provided only for reference. With that context, the authors likely intend the main comparison to be against COLMAP0, for which the table mean is worse than VolSDF. However, the quoted sentence says simply “COLMAP,” while Table 1 also reports COLMAP7 with a better mean Chamfer distance than VolSDF. Because lower Chamfer is better, the unqualified statement is not literally true for all COLMAP results shown. This is a localized comparison-framing issue rather than a methodological flaw, but the text should specify “watertight COLMAP0” or otherwise qualify the claim.

comments.title: BlendedMVS comparison alternates between NeRF++ and NeRF
comments.quote: Therefore we use NeRF++ [39] as a baseline for this dataset. In Table 2 we present our results compared to NeRF++. Qualitative comparisons are presented in Fig. 5; since the units are unknown in this case we present relative improvement of Chamfer distance (in %) compared to NeRF.
comments.explanation: The setup for BlendedMVS consistently identifies NeRF++ as the relevant baseline, which makes sense because the dataset has complex backgrounds. The final phrase then says the Chamfer improvement is compared to NeRF. I checked whether this could simply be OCR dropping the “++”; that is plausible, and the surrounding context strongly suggests NeRF++ was intended. Still, as written, the baseline for the reported relative Chamfer improvement is ambiguous. Since the table reports only percentages rather than absolute Chamfer distances, the reader cannot independently infer from the table whether the denominator is NeRF or NeRF++.

comments.title: NeRF “with normal” baseline is underspecified because NeRF has no SDF normal
comments.quote: Second, by switching the radiance fields of trained NeRF models with an identical radiance field model to ours, namely _Lψ_ ( _**x** ,_ _**n** ,_ _**v** ,_ _**z**_ ).
comments.explanation: I initially read this as an attempt to control for architecture: perhaps NeRF fails only because its original color head is too limited, so the authors give it the same radiance inputs as VolSDF. That is a useful comparison. However, after checking the earlier definitions, _**n**_ in VolSDF is specifically the level-set normal _\nabla_x d_\Omega(x)_, made meaningful by the SDF representation. A standard NeRF density field does not define such a level-set normal in the same way. The authors may have computed a proxy normal from the density gradient, an isosurface normal, or some other construction, but this is not stated here. Those choices are not equivalent and could substantially affect the fairness and reproducibility of the comparison. If the normal is omitted, the model is not actually identical to VolSDF’s radiance model; if a density-gradient normal is used, the paper should say so and clarify how it is normalized and differentiated.

comments.title: Switching only _Lψ_ does not by itself establish a clean appearance/geometry disentanglement
comments.quote: We have tested the disentanglement of scenes to geometry (density) and appearance (radiance field) by switching the radiance fields of two trained scenes. For VolSDF we switched _Lψ_ .
comments.explanation: The intended experiment is clear: keep one scene’s geometry/density and replace its appearance network with another scene’s radiance network. This is a reasonable qualitative probe. But in the model definition, the radiance network receives not only _x_, _n_, and _v_, but also the learned feature _z(x)_ produced by the geometry MLP _f_\phi_. Since _z_ is not constrained to encode only geometry, it could also carry scene-specific appearance or material information. Thus, switching only _L_\psi while leaving _f_\phi and its feature field _z_ fixed does not cleanly switch all appearance-related information. The result may still demonstrate useful empirical modularity, but the stated interpretation that geometry is density and appearance is the radiance field is stronger than what the architecture guarantees. A reader trying to reproduce or interpret the experiment would need to know whether _z_ was treated as part of geometry, appearance, or a shared latent interface, and whether any controls verified that _z_ is not carrying material/color information.

comments.title: Attribution of NeRF’s disentanglement failure specifically to the SDF-density inductive bias is not fully isolated
comments.quote: As shown in Figure 7 both versions of NeRF fail to produce a correct disentanglement in these scenes, while VolSDF successfully switches the materials of the two objects. We attribute this to the specific inductive bias injected with the use of the density in equation 2.
comments.explanation: The qualitative result described here is plausible and consistent with the paper’s overall thesis: defining density as a transformed SDF should encourage a cleaner geometry/appearance separation than an unconstrained NeRF density. Still, the causal attribution is somewhat stronger than the evidence in this passage supports. VolSDF differs from NeRF not only in the density parameterization of equation 2, but also in the SDF regularization, normal definition, sampling procedure, architectural feature interface, and potentially training dynamics. The authors do include a NeRF variant with a VolSDF-like radiance model, which helps, but without further ablations it does not isolate the density transform alone as the reason for successful material transfer. This is best read as a hypothesis supported by the example rather than a demonstrated causal conclusion.

comments.title: Conclusion slightly overstates the demonstrated disentanglement result
comments.quote: This seemingly simple definition provides a useful inductive bias, allowing disentanglement of geometry (i.e., density) and radiance field, and improves the geometry approximation over previous neural volume rendering techniques.
comments.explanation: The paper has provided a principled density construction and a qualitative radiance-switching experiment, so the conclusion is directionally well motivated. My concern is with the word “allowing” in combination with “disentanglement of geometry ... and radiance field,” which can be read as a general property of the formulation. From the passage, the disentanglement evidence is limited to switching radiance networks between trained scenes and observing plausible material transfer. Moreover, because the radiance network is conditioned on the geometry-network feature _z_, the architecture does not formally enforce a separation between appearance and geometry. A more cautious phrasing, such as “encouraging” or “facilitating” disentanglement, would better match what has been shown.
